MINI MINUTES FOR AUG-23-2017 09:30 AM FOR DEPT 302

LAW AND MOTION, DEPT. 302, AS TO DEFENDANT ROBERT LEATHERN'S DEMURRER TO PLAINTIFF SURAJ RAJWANI'S COMPLAINT, THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING. THE DEMURRER IS OVERRULED AS TO THE FIRST THROUGH FOURTH CAUSES OF ACTION AND SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND AS TO THE FIFTH CAUSE OF ACTION. PLAINTIFF'S COUNSEL SUBMITS A PROPOSED ORDER AFTER THE HEARING FOR THE COURT'S REVIEW. JUDGE: CHARLENE P. KIESSELBACH, CLERK: M. GOODMAN, COURT REPORTER: GORDON AIAVAO, CSR #11216, GAIAVAO@SFTC.ORG (302/CPK)

RULING - A R G U E D. Defendant argues. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT ROBERT LEATHERN'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, Alternatively, Motion For Summary Adjudication.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.Defendant Robert Leathern's motion for summary judgment or alternatively summary adjudication is denied in its entirety. Mr. Leathern fails to show that he is entitled to judgment as a matter of law in his favor as to any of the four causes of action alleged against him on any of the many grounds that he has asserted in his moving papers. Mr. Leathern fails to show that the parties had an oral agreement and the two-year statute of limitations applies. The October 16, 2013 e-mail sets forth Mr. Leathern's obligations and plaintiff Suraj Rajwani's promise to consent to the merger. The golden parachute can be implied from that agreement. Mr. Rajwani accepted the agreement by consenting to the merger and golden parachute. There is a triable issue whether the 3-year statute of limitations bars the fraud claim. Mr. Leathern informed Mr. Rajwani that he would not perform in November 2013. Mr. Leathern, however, ignores Mr. Rajwani's allegations and evidence regarding the parties' attempt to locate a nominee that would purchase the shares on Mr. Leathern's behalf. Paragraphs 33-38 of Mr. Rajwani's declaration create a triable issue whether he learned of the facts underlying the fraud claim in May 2014. There is a triable dispute whether the release bars Mr. Rajwani's claims. The release related to the merger and the release of Optimal, HoldCo and Brand Networks and its officers/directors. The agreement between the parties was a confidential side deal unrelated to that transaction. Mr. Leathern was not acting as an officer of the entities when he made the side deal. Mr. Leathern's text that "Yes but to be clear this is between you and I and not involving the company cool?" supports this finding. (Mr. Leathern's understanding of the parties' side agreement before a dispute arose is probative. (So. Cal. Edison Co. v. Sup. Crt. (1995) 37 Cal.App.4th 839, 851.) Mr. Lethern's consideration argument lacks merit. "[A]n option without consideration is not binding on either party until exercised; until then, the option " 'is simply a continuing offer which may be revoked at any time.'" (Steiner v. Thexton (2010) 48 Cal.4th 411, 420 (citation omitted)). Mr. Rajwani exercised the option before revocation. In addition, Mr. Rajwani's agreement to the golden parachute could constitute sufficient consideration. Mr. Leathern admitted that the golden parachute or "280G Consent" was an issue separate from the merger. In other words, the golden parachute approval could be viewed as distinct consideration. Mr. Leathern fails to show that Mr. Rajwani did not incur damage. The economic loss rule does not bar fraud claims. (Erlich v. Menezes (1999) 21 Cal.4th 543, 551-552.) Mr. Leathern fails to show a lack of justifiable reliance and Mr. Rajwani's forbearance could constitute reliance.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Aug-23-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Jeff Kirk, Esq. (415-693-9960) for Plaintiff/ opposing party .
Keith Klein, Esq. (310-576-2100) for Defendant/Moving party ROBERT LEATHERN.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT ROBERT LEATHERN'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

Defendant Robert Leathern's demurrer to plaintiff Suraj Rajwani's complaint is overruled as to the first through fourth causes of action and sustained with leave to amend as to the fifth cause of action. Mr. Leathern has not shown that the first, second, third, and fourth causes of action are barred by their respective statutes of limitations. The running of the statute must appear "clearly and affirmatively" from the face of the complaint, and it is not enough that the complaint might be time-barred. (Committee for Green Foothills v. Santa Clara County Bd. of Supervisors (2010) 48 Cal.4th 32, 42; Geneva Towers Ltd. Partnership v. City and County of San Francisco (2003) 29 Cal.4th 769, 781.) Paragraph 9 of the complaint states that Mr. Leathern confirmed the parties' verbal agreement in writing by sending an email on October 16, 2013 indicating that Mr. Leathern would confidentially buy Mr. Rajwani's HoldCo shares between October 16, 2013 and April 16, 2014. Liberally construing the complaint, it cannot be determined that this writing is insufficient for purposes of the statute of limitations. Accordingly, the first, second, and third causes of action for specific performance, breach of contract, and breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing are not time-barred because they are subject to the four-year statute of limitations set forth in CCP Sec. 337(1). As to the fourth cause of action, Mr. Leathern has not shown that the fraud claim is time-barred pursuant to CCP Sec. 338(d). It does not appear clearly and affirmatively from the face of the complaint that the statute of limitations expired on December 13, 2016 as Mr. Leathern contends. The complaint is silent as to when Mr. Rajwani discovered the facts constituting the fraud or mistake, and paragraph 17 of the complaint seems to indicate such discovery was on May 17, 2014 when Mr. Rajwani learned that the new buyer withdrew because Mr. Leathern refused to consent to the sale. Mr. Rajwani fails to substantively oppose the demurrer to the fifth cause of action and his failure to do is treated as a concession that the demurrer to that claim is meritorious. If he can do so in good faith, Mr. Rajwani is granted twenty days leave to amend to specify whether the loan agreement was written or oral and to show why the claim is not time-barred.(as fully reported in the Reporter's notes) Plaintiff's Counsel submits a proposed order after the hearing for the Court's review.
